title: Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns

learningObjectives.text: Explain components as building blocks using a new example and justify the result.
Apply jsx syntax and purpose using a new example and justify the result.
Explain react's separation-of-concerns model using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in components, jsx, and separation of concerns recur throughout later React work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **Components, JSX, and Props** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Components as building blocks** → **JSX syntax and purpose** → **React's separation-of-concerns model**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Components as building blocks
sections.body: Components divide an interface into reusable, composable units. A useful component owns one coherent responsibility and accepts explicit inputs.

sections.title: JSX syntax and purpose
sections.body: JSX is JavaScript syntax for describing element trees. Expressions appear in braces, attributes use React-oriented names, and JSX compiles to element creation calls.

sections.title: React's separation-of-concerns model
sections.body: React often colocates JSX with the state and behavior that determine it. Concerns are separated by component responsibility rather than file type alone.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: JSX describes an element tree and the function supplies a reusable boundary.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns connects Components as building blocks, JSX syntax and purpose, React's separation-of-concerns model.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Components as building blocks” in the context of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns?

choices.label: A library provides focused capabilities while an application chooses broader structure; a framework supplies more conventions and lifecycle.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components as building blocks.

choices.label: Ref values persist for the component instance but changes are invisible to rendering.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components as building blocks.

choices.label: Components divide an interface into reusable, composable units. A useful component owns one coherent responsibility and accepts explicit inputs.
choices.feedback: Components as building blocks is best understood as follows: Components divide an interface into reusable, composable units. A useful component owns one coherent responsibility and accepts explicit inputs.

choices.label: UI dispatches an action, reducers calculate state, and subscribed UI reads the new state.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components as building blocks.

questions.explanation: Components as building blocks is best understood as follows: Components divide an interface into reusable, composable units. A useful component owns one coherent responsibility and accepts explicit inputs.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Apply Components as building blocks deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns?

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Components as building blocks, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns?

choices.label: Use Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

choices.label: Avoid Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

choices.label: Use Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
